What matters is not whether a region is insulated, but whether its contribution to perception is stable and specific when engaged.

Under this view, the mind contains pockets of modularity, with selective processing embedded within broader, multiplex systems. Constraint is local and partial, expressed through selective causal influence rather than through global encapsulation.

Network modularity

The classical view of the brain as a collection of separately functioning modules has been replaced by a network perspective in which cognition arises from interactions among distributed subsystems (Boerger et al., 2023). In this framework, the brain is composed of anatomical and functional nodes whose patterns of interaction underlie behavior and thought.

Modularity is no longer defined as a psychological property of systems, but as a statistical property of networks. It quantifies how tightly nodes connect within communities relative to across them. Canonical networks such as visual, salience, limbic, and default-mode systems show reproducible patterns of co-activation across tasks and individuals (Figure 6). At this scale, the brain is well described as a hierarchically modular network.

This reframing preserves what modularity was always meant to capture: segregation under constraint. Local clusters form tight communities through dense internal connectivity and sparse external links. The result is a supra-network organized into nested modules, subnetworks within networks, modules within modules (Meunier et al., 2010; Seguin et al., 2023). These modules are scale-dependent, and structural and functional partitions need not align. Different parcellations or resolutions yield different community structures. Modules also reconfigure with learning, arousal, and task demands, which means that the relevant unit is often a transient subgraph with a distinctive dynamical signature rather than a fixed anatomical parcel.

Figure 6. Anatomical topographies of canonical large-scale networks. Adapted from Boerger et al., 2023.

Topological modularity confers computational advantages to a network. Dense local clustering supports fast and specialized processing at low wiring cost, while a limited number of long-range tracts preserves short communication paths and global integration. Balancing clustering with integration yields small-world organization, combining high local efficiency with selective long-range shortcuts that sustain rapid coordination.

This balance allows information to remain locally encapsulated without enforcing global isolation. Information redundancy can be tolerated without global spread, and modules can be reconfigured as task demands change. Some communities remain highly specialized, while others act as hubs coordinating distributed activity. Bottom-up and top-down influences emerge from this organization rather than being imposed by design. This architecture is best understood as the product of coevolution between structural constraints and dynamical demands (Figure 7).

Figure 7. Community structure of modules within submodules illustrated in very large-scale integrated circuit (A), C. elegans (B), human MRI (C) and human diffusion spectrum imaging (D). « Many systems have the fractal property of hierarchical modularity, multi-scale modularity or russian doll modularity ». Adapted from Meunier, Lambiotte and Bullmore, 2010.

Economy further constrains both architecture and signaling. Neural tissue occupies space. Long axons are metabolically costly, and cranial volume limits distance. The brain therefore trades wiring cost against communicative efficiency (Bullmore and Sporns, 2012). Lattice-like networks are cheap but slow; random networks are fast but expensive. Small-world architectures achieve high local efficiency while limiting the number of costly long-range connections. The same trade-off shapes information flow. Under structural and energetic constraints, sparse coding and prediction-error minimization reduce signaling load. Signals may follow short paths, traverse polysynaptic relays, or diffuse through broadcast routes. Which route dominates depends on task demands, network topology, and physiological state (Figure 8).

Figure 8. Signal propagation within brain networks satisfy distinct needs, shaped by task, topology and physiological state. Adapted from Seguin et al., 2023.

Under this interpretation, modularity does not compete with predictive processing. It supplies the architectural constraints that make prediction efficient and selective. Priors and prediction errors circulate through specific channels and bottlenecks rather than globally. Pockets of modularity correspond to subgraphs that are not only internally cohesive but also causally efficacious. Perturbing them alters perception or behavior in ways that cannot be reproduced by perturbing other, equally sized sets of nodes. Constraint is enforced by network organization.

The mind, the brain and the network

Bridging structure and function requires a framework that treats anatomy, or topology, as a constraint on how information flows. We can map which neurons connect and which fire together, yet this remains insufficient. We need an account of how mesoscale architecture constrains the dynamics that are actually realized.

One way to make these constraints explicit is to represent networks in terms of higher-order relations that extend connectivity beyond pairs (Reimann et al., 2017). Groups of fully interconnected neurons form simplices such as lines, triangles, and tetrahedra. When these simplices interlock and leave gaps, the resulting cavities highlight the scaffolds through which activity circulates. Because neurons can belong to several simplices whose configurations evolve over time, participation across cavities becomes a dynamic signature of computation. Cavities describe how local channels of flow combine into larger trajectories of activity.

Figure 9. Cliques are local neighborhoods in structural brain networks; a single neuron can join multiple cliques. Cavities are joints of cliques that appear as voids enclosed by simplices, then disappear as new connections fill them. Long-lived cavities show that the connectome is organized hierarchically in higher-order relations, rather than just edges and triangles. Adapted from Sizemore et al., 2017.

Under this view, modularity is not a matter of partitioning the brain into boxes, but a problem of dynamical geometry. Anatomical wiring determines which cliques and cavities can exist; topology determines how they constrain the propagation of signals through time. When a circuit is stimulated, activity unfolds in reproducible sequences: small cliques activate first, merge into higher-order cavities, and then collapse in an orderly pattern. These trajectories show that cortical organization is dynamic. Topological features predict the order, reach, and coordination of neural activity. Early processing can remain locally insulated within cliques while still being shaped at the mesoscale by how cavities open and close upstream and downstream.

Figure 10. As activity propagates, small groups of neurons fire together and form fully connected cliques (simplices). When these cliques overlap, they bound a cavity (a higher-dimensional void). As additional neurons are recruited, more cliques form and eventually fill in the cavity. Shapes fill in and then fill out in sequence, producing a “rain” of high-dimensional cavities across time and regions. Adapted from Reimann et al., 2017.

Any theory of cognition that treats modularity as static partitioning, or information flow as reducible to pairwise connectivity, will fail to explain how selective influence, stability, and efficiency coexist in systems that are both plastic and inferential. Constraint is not enforced by encapsulation alone, nor by global prediction error minimization. It is enforced by the geometry of admissible trajectories through the network.

Topological properties preserve selectivity, regulate bottom-up and top-down influence, and maintain efficiency in information flow (features once attributed to inferential systems or psychological modules). Directed cliques implement local computation. Cavities integrate these computations into mesoscale assemblies whose formation and dissolution define the temporal structure of processing.

Figure 11. Cliques reflect local feedforward motifs – tight neighborhoods of interacting neurons. Cavities capture how such cliques relate to each other. A high β₁ (many one-dimensional loops) indicates distributed cycles of activity; a high β₃ (many three-dimensional voids bounded by tetrahedra) indicates strongly coordinated ensembles. Tracking cliques reveals local information flow; tracking cavities reveals global scaffolding of communication. Adapted from Reimann et al., 2017.

Structural connectivity alone can’t determine how information travels. Signals may follow short paths, traverse polysynaptic relays, or diffuse through broadcast processes. Which route dominates depends on topology, physiological state, and task demands. By capturing the nested organization (modularity) through which activity flows, merges, and dissipates, we can explain how a structure shaped by metabolic cost and predictive constraints yields reproducible trajectories of communication. In this sense, structure predicts dynamics because topology governs the flow.

Taken together, these considerations show that the classical opposition between connectionism and modularity does not identify competing explanations, but competing attempts to express the same requirement: learning must operate under constraint. Perception is not theory-neutral as it gest penetrated, yet early processing remains locally stable. The brain is structured with specialized modules at the routing level. Rather than asking whether cognition is modular or distributed, the relevant question is which topological constraints make certain patterns of learning, influence, and integration possible, and others impossible.

References

Boerger, T. F., Pahapill, P., Butts, A. M., Arocho-Quinones, E., Raghavan, M., & Krucoff, M. O. (2023). Large-scale brain networks and intra-axial tumor surgery: a narrative review of functional mapping techniques, critical needs, and scientific opportunities. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 17, 1170419.

Bornkessel-Schlesewsky, I., & Schlesewsky, M. (2019). Toward a neurobiologically plausible model of language-related, negative event-related potentials. Frontiers in psychology, 10, 298.

Bullmore, E., & Sporns, O. (2012). The economy of brain network organization. Nature reviews neuroscience, 13(5), 336-349.

Clark, A. (2013). Whatever next? Predictive brains, situated agents, and the future of cognitive science. Behavioral and brain sciences, 36(3), 181-204.

De Ladurantaye, V., Rouat, J., & Vanden-Abeele, J. (2012). Models of Information Processing. Visual Cortex: Current Status and Perspectives, 227.

Fodor, J. A. (1983). The modularity of mind. MIT press.

Gomez, J., Barnett, M., & Grill-Spector, K. (2019). Extensive childhood experience with Pokémon suggests eccentricity drives organization of visual cortex. Nature human behaviour, 3(6), 611-624.

Herzog, M. H., & Clarke, A. M. (2014). Why vision is not both hierarchical and feedforward. Frontiers in computational neuroscience, 8, 135.

Hosoya, T., Baccus, S. A., & Meister, M. (2005). Dynamic predictive coding by the retina. Nature, 436(7047), 71-77.

Lupyan, G. (2015). Cognitive penetrability of perception in the age of prediction: Predictive systems are penetrable systems. Review of philosophy and psychology, 6(4), 547-569.

Meunier, D., Lambiotte, R., & Bullmore, E. T. (2010). Modular and hierarchically modular organization of brain networks. Frontiers in neuroscience, 4, 200.

Moro, S. S., Qureshi, F. A., & Steeves, J. K. (2023). Perception of the McGurk effect in people with one eye depends on whether the eye is removed during infancy or adulthood. Frontiers in Neuroscience, 17, 1217831.

Pylyshyn, Z. (1999). Is vision continuous with cognition?: The case for cognitive impenetrability of visual perception. Behavioral and brain sciences, 22(3), 341-365.

Reimann, M. W., Nolte, M., Scolamiero, M., Turner, K., Perin, R., Chindemi, G., ... & Markram, H. (2017). Cliques of neurons bound into cavities provide a missing link between structure and function. Frontiers in computational neuroscience, 11, 266051.

Schalk, G., Kapeller, C., Guger, C., Ogawa, H., Hiroshima, S., Lafer-Sousa, R., ... & Kanwisher, N. (2017). Facephenes and rainbows: Causal evidence for functional and anatomical specificity of face and color processing in the human brain. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 114(46), 12285-12290.

Seguin, C., Jedynak, M., David, O., Mansour, S., Sporns, O., & Zalesky, A. (2023). Communication dynamics in the human connectome shape the cortex-wide propagation of direct electrical stimulation. Neuron, 111(9), 1391-1401.

Sizemore, A. E., Giusti, C., Kahn, A., Vettel, J. M., Betzel, R. F., & Bassett, D. S. (2018). Cliques and cavities in the human connectome. Journal of computational neuroscience, 44(1), 115-145.

item.title: Can machines think? 2.0
item.description: Assessing machine vs human cognition in
item.dc:creator: Valentin Guigon
item.content:encoded:
We have been studying human and machine cognition for more than seventy years. Mostly uninterested in the artificiality/biologicalness of its physical support, Cognition is comprehended as information processing that can be decomposed into formal systems that represent things and transform them. Turing’s imitation game (1950) has been way too much discussed. Turing (1950) has not been enough discussed in regard to the rest of the cognitivist heritage. Here, I confront Turing (1950) and Marr (1982). If Turing’s test 1.0 was about imitating humans in verbal exchanges, Turing’s test 2.0 must be about playing Baseball and competing in Poker.
Equivalences accepted.

Turing and the operationalization of thinking

Addressing cognition can be summarized, in its most primary form, as answering the question “What is thinking?”. Alan Turing sought to answer the follow-up question “Can machines think?” in his famous paper Computing Machinery and Intelligence.

Turing operationalized the vague metaphysical question into a testable one: “Could a universal digital computer, properly resourced and programmed, imitate a human well enough in conversation?” His imitation game evaluated thinking through externally observable behavior, specifically symbolic dialogue. In this game, an interrogator attempts to distinguish between two hidden entities – a human and a machine – on the basis of written responses. Success for the machine is not defined by replicating human cognition but by achieving behavioral indistinguishability.

This operationalization established a methodological template that is still shaping AI testing. For many engineers, the best hopes of reaching Artificial General Intelligence rest on benchmarks where performance is assessed by whether outputs approximate or outperform human responses in dialogue, summarization, or reasoning tasks. Success remains defined in terms of symbol manipulation – embeddings seem to be the machine equivalent of human engrams.

Critiques of Turing’s Operationalization

This operationalization is pretty powerful. Equating “thinking” with “imitating humans” provides a test through behavior alone, disregarding mental processes just as behaviorists disregarded the black box. We apply roughly the same logics in animal research when we infer internal processes from observable actions – animals cannot fulfill questionnaires. Ultimately, subjective experience is not easily captured through behavioral proxies, and cannot be communicated without ambiguity.

Turing also chose not to evaluate machines on “racing” capabilities, nor or IQ-style problem solving (early 20th century). However, while IQ tests can be reduced to symbolic manipulation, what Turing calls racing capabilities cannot. Consider a baseball player catching a fly ball: the player must compute distance and angle, predict trajectory, and adjust the body’s motion. This can be solved by calculus-like derivations or by heuristics such as maintaining a constant angle between the ball and the look. Either way, it involves processing information and predicting outcomes – hence, thinking. By excluding such cases, Turing privileged symbolic tasks, and ignored a large portion of cognition involving bodily states.

This framing has further consequences. While animals may manipulate symbols, Turing’s test rejects non-linguistic forms of problem solving that we found in animals. Machines also diverge in regard to the information streams. They receive non-sparse data streams, they receive it when prompted by a higher-level agent, and they solve optimization problems for estimation purposes. Humans sample information sparsely for inference purposes, at all time, minimizing energy costs within bodily and environmental constraints. Though, whether real cognition requires embodiment remains debated.

That said, to reach a glass of water partially hidden by a forefront object, we can hardly equate a robot hand using multiple layers of transformer architectures to solve 2d-to-3d mapping for planning the trajectory, with c. elegans solving orientation towards the glass in 3d spaces with mechanosensory and proprioceptive information. The latter use a simpler architecture, more complex body-environment interactions, richer information, and can be explained with a tractable number of features. They have 302 neurons and 95 muscle cells. For that reason, I do think a modern version of the Turing test should account for non-linguistic forms of problem solving.

The Imitation game: A game of Game Theory

Other critics involve thought experiments like the Chinese Room, arguing that syntactic manipulation of symbols does not equal semantic understanding. Let’s remain empirically grounded:

The imitation game involves deception, thus strategic interactions. Solving strategic interactions typically requires backward induction, payoff matrices, incomplete information, and theory of mind reasoning. Humans approximate such reasoning with inductions and bounded rationality. How would machines do?

Consider the 11-20 game, where best replies cycle and no pure Nash equilibrium exists. Humans’ responses are distributed between 17-18-19 in a large majority, and solving the game requires a Bayesian strategy whereby one should answer 15-16-17 with 20-25% probability. Humans iterate through backward induction based on their most probable belief about others’ beliefs. Consider an LLM in reasoning mode solving the 11-20 game. If the LLM outputs tokens that describe some form of theory of mind when they iterate through levels of reasoning (simulating depths of thinking, or perhaps just convoluted reasoning) because Ayad and Rubinstein (2012) was present in its training database, is that “imitating humans”? Is ChatGPT-5 response to choose 15 with high probability the result of bounded backward induction? Tokens are chosen by matter of logits; is the most probable token equivalent to the most probable belief in a human being?

Then, what if the LLM instead iterates until the lowest bound?

In the Ultimatum game, human players of both roles tend to naturally converge on the 50/50 division. A reasoning LLM that has been trained on game-theory textbooks can carry out full proper backward induction and articulate reasoning across several depths. Proper backward induction would lead the models to propose the minimal positive split (99/1), because it is a perfect equilibrium. On the other hand, knowledge about decision under uncertainty leads to avoid offers that create a meaningful rejection risk for a small marginal gain. Humans naïve of experimental economics reason in steps — they rarely go beyond two or three steps. In sequential games with incomplete information, many human players truncate recursions, mis-specify beliefs, stop at a shallow depth and especially rely on (optimal) heuristics. Humans form beliefs that conform to k-levels of reasoning for the reason they are bounded. An LLM that prints the full chain of expected-utility computations is solving normatively. In such a case, its offer would very likely be rejected for unfairness. Simple tests show they are able to provide a 50/50 split. Is it for being trained with data on bounded rationality and k-levels of reasoning, for existence of bounded rationality, for existence of proper k-levels of reasoning, or for both?

Ultimately, to imitate human thinking across multiple strategic settings, a model would need to reproduce humans’ limited depth, noise, heterogeneous priors, and heuristics. This circles back to Turing’s insight that passing the imitation game requires matching human errors and biases. In case the machine is competing against a competent human opponent with knowledge of game theory that deceives the interrogator by providing non-bounded-rationality answer, can we equate the machine’s ability to human thinking?

Child programs with learning is all we need

Regardless, modern LLMs do replicate human-like outputs. They can answer questions, summarize, and engage in reasoning-like tasks. They produce text that can deceive human judges, showing that discrete-state machines and the Turing Test were well anticipated. Yet we quickly learnt to spot them through characteristic stylistic patterns. On the other side, humans can keep deceiving one another continuously. This shows that Turing’s test is incomplete. Performance assessed via symbolic representations at a given moment is insufficient to characterize stable feats of intelligence comparative to that of humans.

Turing included in his paper the clause that a true test of the imitation game would require “child machines” that learn through experience, rather than being pre-loaded with exhaustive training. The task ahead was, and remains, to construct child machines capable of learning through experience, education, and reinforcement.

The Imitation game 2.0: A game of Baseball-Poker

This raises the broader issue that defining machine counterparts of human cognitive functions (vision, reasoning, language, emotions) is always arbitrary. We get deceived by LLM’s large context windows, , whereby they seem to learn about us personally and display a wide range of behaviors. Let’s instead consider an LLM with the ability to retrain on the fly its logits. If cognition is an ensemble of classes (cognitive functions), each described by an ensemble of diverse processes, rather than a single class of symbolic manipulations, then any imitation benchmark risks being partial.

The concern is not whether machines truly “think” like humans but to properly define the threshold at which machines imitate humans well enough to pass as comparable. To improve the cognitive science program, the imitation game 2.0 must be a game of racing-deceiving. I’m convinced it should be a game of Baseball-Poker.

Cognitive science requires more than studying outputs

The next step of the cognitive science program may be to reflect on its own goals. What is the purpose of evaluating LLMs on human-like benchmarks — if the outcome is not to prove they think as we do, but to decide when their performance is close enough to count as imitation?

Three decades after Turing, David Marr proposed a framework for analyzing complex information-processing systems. Perception, he argued, and by extension cognition, can’t be understood by reducing systems to their elementary parts alone. Complex systems require descriptions across multiple scales (neurons, neural ensembles, areas, loops, systems).

Importantly for what is at stake here, Marr addressed processes rather than loci. He distinguished three levels for analyzing cognition: the computational (what problem is being solved and why), the algorithmic (how representations are manipulated), and the implementational (how processes are physically realized). In theory, while the three levels of analysis are independent and one can study one level alone, two levels at once strengthen the response to objections and the integration of three levels at once is required to ensure a full understanding. In practice, the three levels may be hierarchical, with dysfunction at the implementational level constraining higher-level processes.

Marr addressed processes, not outputs.

For instance: Bayesian models describe cognition as probabilistic inference. At the computational level, they formalize inference under uncertainty; at the algorithmic level, they specify Bayesian estimation; at the implementational level, they explore neural coding of probability distributions. Whether the brain literally computes probabilities – meaning that neurons compute probabilities – remains debated. One would need to address the three levels at once to resolve the question; much work stays at the computational and algorithmic levels, without bridging to implementation.

This highlights a distinction between instrumental models, which prioritize predictive utility, and mechanistic models, which seek biological accuracy. Marr himself, as he should, seemed to emphasize explanatory adequacy. Explaining processes via modelling is necessary to approximate biological reality and, potentially, clinical intervention. For him, a model must capture a sufficient range of the processes it aims to explain in order to count as a proper explanation.

What does it have to do with Turing’s test?

Turing argues that the fly controls its flight through a collection of about five systems, and three processes account for probably about 60% of the fly vision. Let’s consider that a model of the fly that can account for about sixty percent of its vision begins to have explanatory value. Applying this logic to machines such as large language models, we may ask: if they display linguistic competence but lack other essential capacities, such as motor control or perceptual processing, have we achieved a good-enough model of intelligence?

Vision and language remain the dominant focus of AI evaluation. Unlike perception or syntax, domains such as motor control, decision-making, or emotion are less straightforward to formalize. Yet humans and animals solve these problems through input-output transformations. Some animals solve similar problems with different strategies; others solve entirely different problems. We often recognize certain animal capacities as “thinking,” while relegating others to “mechanistic” behavior. Yet the threshold between mechanistic response and cognition remains unclear. For this reason, and despite passing Turing’s test 1.0, the same ambiguity shapes our evaluation of machines.

Concluding reflections

Turing framed intelligence not as a single object but as a performance criterion: could machines imitate humans well enough to deceive us? This instrumental perspective launched a great research program, but it left aside how cognition is physically realized. It aimed to care only about the capacity to imitate. To what level should we consider imitation to be a success? Is mapping a machine output (yhat) to a human output (y) with a success rate above chance-level sufficient?

Marr, by contrast, emphasized explanation through modelling processes across computational, algorithmic, and implementational levels. A model only counts as explanatory if it captures a sufficient range of the processes it claims to represent. In Turing’s terms, programming a system to reproduce human behavior without physical implementation may suffice to win the imitation game. In Marr’s terms it leaves out the implementational level.

The two approaches are not opposed. Instrumental successes can serve as stepping stones toward mechanistic models. But if our goal is explanation, we cannot remain satisfied with models that only simulate outputs.

Resources

  • Turing, A.M. (1950). Computing machinery and intelligence. Mind, 59, 433-460.

  • Marr, D. (1982). Vision. In R. Cummins & D. D. Cummins (Eds.), Minds, Brains, and Computers: The Foundations of Cognitive Science (pp. 69–83). Blackwell Publishers.

  • Gigerenzer, G., & Brighton, H. (2009). Homo heuristicus: Why biased minds make better inferences. Topics in cognitive science, 1(1), 107-143.

  • Arad, A., & Rubinstein, A. (2012). The 11–20 money request game: A level-k reasoning study. American Economic Review, 102(7), 3561-3573.

item.title: Neuroscience 2035: What the next decade looks like
item.description: The previous decade (2015-2025) has been busy building tools, methods, and diagnoses. This new decade will be integrative.
item.dc:creator: Valentin Guigon
item.content:encoded:
This is a living post. It is not intended to be exhaustive. It is intended to document the major transformations of the field, from 2025 to 2035. Last update: February 2026.

Starting with the premise that the next decade of Neuroscience will be about integration, what follows is an effort to identify major trends, conjecture constraints, and monitor advances to take the remaining steps.

Subscribe now

The next decade of Neuroscience

Progress will be organized around five axes.

Axis 1: New techniques and technologies

The two most consequential technical developments of the next decade are patient- and circuit-specific generative models — digital twins — and the systematic integration of cross-species behavioral platforms.

By digital twins I mean formal generative models parameterized to a specific patient or circuit that can be used for in silico stress testing, intervention planning, and drug-development triage. This includes lab-level twins in which one can simulate fMRI, EEG, or intracranial signals and simulate agents performing tasks, probing what a model predicts before committing to an experiment. The appeal of models in siloco is its possibility to investigate the space of possible interventions that is too large to explore empirically. Technically, these models should aim at simulating predictions by encoding testable structures; where modeling aims at fitting empirical observations. Useful digital twins interpolate data and extrapolate to perturbations. The model can be extended to the digital simulation of an entire lab (see Gilad Kusne closed-loop discovery for an example in materials science).

Complementary to digital twins, cross-species comparison and in silico modeling are converging toward a shared function: reducing dependence on animal experiments without losing mechanistic resolution. Digital twins parameterized on human data can be stress-tested against perturbations that would require animal models to run empirically; cross-species platforms establish where animal findings generalize and where they don’t. Used together, in silico models could operate as a bridge between species.

On the cross-species side, a recent piece in The Transmitter, “Neuroscience has a species problem,” observes that the field has never been richer in data, yet it remains organized along species lines. Animal and human researchers often operate within separate conceptual frameworks, attend different conferences, and develop theories that rarely confront data across species. The BRAIN Roadmap dedicate an entire innovation domain to find technical solutions to the problem. A parallel development of organoid and computer models are raising the prospect of eliminating some animal experiments (cf., Diana Kwon’s news feature in Nature, 2026).

The hardware is also changing. Advances in materials science and AI-assisted materials discovery are producing new substrates for neural interfaces: more biocompatible, higher-density, lower-noise electrode arrays; flexible and conformable probes that reduce tissue damage; non-invasive imaging modalities with resolution approaching that of invasive methods (cf., BRAIN initiative advances).

BCI development is accelerating on both the invasive and non-invasive sides (e.g., Blackrock Neurotech, OpenBCI, Synchron, INBRAIN Neuroelectronics, Precision Neuroscience, IAI by Biotech Dental). High-channel-count implants are expanding beyond severely motor-impaired populations toward broader clinical and eventually research applications. On the non-invasive side, transcranial methods are gaining temporal and spatial precision. The next decade will see BCIs move from assistive devices for a narrow population toward research instruments capable of probing circuit-level dynamics in a wider range of participants and conditions. It is contingent on the governance frameworks discussed in Axis 2, toward clinical tools for psychiatric and neurological intervention.

At the molecular level, AI-assisted protein structure prediction (e.g., AlphaFold) and systematic genomic analysis are opening the drug discovery pipeline in ways that were not tractable five years ago. Identifying candidate molecules for circuit-level interventions, discovering which genomic variants correspond to which computational phenotypes, and mapping the proteome of specific cell types to intervention targets are becoming computationally tractable.

Beyond identifying variants associated with disorder risk, the next decade will see AI-assisted approaches capable of characterizing the combinatorial structure of gene expression across cell types, developmental stages, and disease states at a resolution that hypothesis-driven approaches cannot reach. Neuroscience will meet AI-assisted discovery as a second step to provide sound hypothesis-driven links between genomic configurations and circuit-level phenotypes.

Axis 2: Thematic focus

The thematic core of the next decade is integrating data across levels of description, across species, across data modalities, and across levels of impairment.

Precision psychiatry is one expression of precision neuroscience in the mental health domain. Despite current barriers to including translational tools to computational psychiatry — preventing powerful predictions at the individual level — the field is aiming to discover computational phenotypes, and to characterize at the individual level a specific computational profile. Diagnostic labels (e.g., ASD, ADHD) are the golden standard, but inadequate targets for intervention because they aggregate individuals who differ fundamentally in the processes generating their symptoms. As Feczko and colleagues (2019, Trends in Cognitive Sciences) argued, the heterogeneity problem — the fact that psychiatric categories contain subtypes with distinct biological and computational profiles — is one of the central unsolved problems in translational neuroscience.

Addressing heterogeneity requires moving beyond descriptive phenotyping toward computational phenotyping: stable, cross-context profiles of how an individual processes information, derived from formal behavioral models and integrated with self-report measures, genomic data, and connectivity profiles under explicit reliability constraints. Precedents exists at the neural level. Buch and colleagues (2023, Nature Neuroscience) applied regularized canonical correlation analysis to resting-state functional connectivity in ASD, identifying latent brain-behavior dimensions and reproducible subgroups with distinct connectivity alterations and clinical profiles. The next decade requires bringing the same integration logic to the behavioral and cognitive level, where it is closer to intervention targets. Questionnaires capture stable traits but misaddress mechanisms. Computational model parameters are mechanistic but typically fit task by task, yielding estimates that are context-bound and unstable, as Eckstein and colleagues (2022, eLife) demonstrated. Hitchcock, Fried, and Frank (2022, Annual Review of Psychology) identified poor test-retest reliability, limited ecological validity, and the cost of validation at scale as the primary barriers to translating computational psychiatry. Getting to both stability and mechanistic specificity requires multi-task modeling, hierarchical estimation, and explicit data fusion. None of those is standard yet. Pelin and colleagues (2021, Neuropsychopharmacology) demonstrated the possibility of identifying transdiagnostic psychiatric subtypes through unsupervised learning on integrated data. One of the next challenges is making that generalizable.

The technical prerequisite for computational phenotyping is data fusion and dimensionality reduction. Fusing behavioral parameters, self-report measures, connectivity profiles, and genomic data into stable individual-level representations requires methods that do not yet exist in deployable form. Current dimensionality reduction approaches (canonical correlation analysis, factor analysis, manifold learning) each make assumptions that are not always satisfied when data come from heterogeneous sources collected under different protocols. The phenotypes and diagnostic categories will follow from the development of such methods.

Finally, as human neuroscience datasets expand in scale and precision, they become re-identification risks. A 2026 preprint by Lermen and colleagues demonstrates large-scale online deanonymization using LLMs. This is a warning that neural, behavioral, and self-report data combined are not safely anonymized by current standards. Note that the BRAIN Initiative has made data sovereignty commitments to their roadmap.

Axis 3: Methods and analytical approaches

To meet the methodological demand for integrative neuroscience, uses of ML will shift from a tool for fitting data to infrastructure for building interpretable, generative models of cognition and brain function.

Psychology has systematically prioritized explanation over prediction, producing models that fit the data they were built on but failed to generalize (Yarkoni and Westfall, 2017). At the same time, a model that predicts well but cannot be interpreted does not advance understanding. A system can be behaviorally successful without being a good model of cognition, and cognitively informative without being biologically realistic. As I’ve reminded recently, “thanks to the cyclopean volume of data now available and the computation power to process it, LLMs and other AI systems can use complex, opaque architectures and generalize extremely well.” Criteria for great explanatory models — accuracy in predicting held-out data, generalization across stimuli and tasks, and consistency with known facts about cortical organization and physiology (Brette, 2019; Mathis et al., 2024) — are not currently met by most published models. There is however a coherent research program working on the prediction-interpretation tension through hybrid models that combine the predictive power of deep neural networks with the interpretability of classical cognitive models.

For instance, Sandbrink and Summerfield apply deep neural networks to cognitive flexibility as formal theories of underlying computations — using the DNN as a substrate in which to instantiate and test cognitive hypotheses (see also Xu and colleagues' CogReact framework). Eckstein, Summerfield, Daw, and Miller combined artificial neural networks with classical cognitive models to understand human learning and decision making. Miller and colleagues used RNN structure to discover what cognitive strategies behavioral data are consistent with, keeping the result interpretable by design. Ji-An, Benna, and Mattar used small recurrent neural networks to recover interpretable cognitive strategies from behavioral data. See more attempts here: Glöckner, Jekel, and Lisovoj (ML to evaluate models of probabilistic inference); Plonsky and colleagues (behavioral theories combined with ML to predict human decisions); Eckstein and colleagues (hybrid neural-cognitive models of how memory shapes reward learning).

Overall, the next decade will see increasing effort to build hybrid models where the model components are constrained by neurotransmitter pathways, network circuitry and modular structures. The basal ganglia-dopamine system is one of the best-understood circuits in neuroscience, and has been successfully represented by reinforcement learning architectures. The next decade will see increasing effort to build hybrid models where the RL components are constrained by what is known about dopaminergic learning signals, striatal circuitry, and prefrontal control. Here, NeuroAI will especially benefit molecular precision and systems neuroscience.

The NeuroAI direction will benefit both Neuro-modeling and Ai-modeling. Gradient descent is computationally implausible in biological systems, and decades of attempts to find biologically realistic alternatives have been largely unsuccessful. The search for algorithms that are both more biologically plausible and more efficient, particularly in terms of energy, is on. Work like Jean-Rémy King’s on vision models shows that some deep neural network architectures already fit human behavioral and brain data at high resolution; understanding where they succeed and where they fail will constrain the next generation of models.

Generative AI is also entering the scientific process itself as a hypothesis-generation engine. The systematic search for patterns in large genomic, connectomic, and behavioral datasets is increasingly AI-assisted. The structure of discovery will change: hypotheses emerge from pattern recognition at scale and are then subjected to experimental test, rather than originating from theory or prior observation alone. Whether this produces principled understanding or sophisticated data compression depends on whether the patterns found are interpretable, transportable, and theoretically grounded — which brings it back to the modeling standards above.

Axis 4: Scales of investigation

Scaling in neuroscience is becoming a matter of getting the right data at the right levels simultaneously.

The multi-level problem (connecting molecular events to cellular dynamics to circuit computations to systems-level behavior) requires models that specify what the connection between levels should look like, and data that can test those specifications. Individual variability is becoming a central aspect of modeling research, thanks to the generalization of probabilistic programming and hierarchical predictive structures. Studies like the ABCD Study have generated an enormous longitudinal dataset on brain development across agespans, and offer a superb ground to test multi-level computational modeling. Cross-species comparisons will become another centre of focus for isolating conserved computations from species-specific elaborations. To realize that goal, data acquisition protocols will integrate at the onset the need for behavioral and recording harmonization, and the need for thorough computational modeling.

The current final boss of the scaling issue is integration: fusing data from tasks, questionnaires, connectivity profiles, genomic measures, and longitudinal observations into representations rich enough to support individual-level inference.

Axis 5: Conceptual foundations

The four axes above describe what the field will build and measure. Neuroscience as a fill will also need to rethink its foundations and its theoretical commitments.

The rise of AI as a scientific object has forced a return to questions that cognitive science treated as settled. Embodiment (the dependence of cognition on a body situated in an environment) is re-entering the conversation as a live empirical constraint. Models that learn from static datasets do not interact with the world; models that control bodies in environments do, and their behavior raises questions about whether the representational frameworks developed for disembodied systems remain applicable. The next decade will see increasing pressure to account for sensorimotor coupling, action-perception loops, and developmental trajectory in both AI and neuroscience models.

Questions about representation, intentionality, consciousness, and the conditions under which internal organization warrants cognitive interpretation are appearing in AI safety research, in debates about model evaluation, and in neuroscience discussions about what circuit-level findings actually explain. This renewal is partly productive and partly confused. The concepts are old and the empirical context is new. The communities involved seem to rarely share a common framework for what would count as evidence.

As I’ve recently argued, the field needs is a principled approach to cognitive interpretability: criteria for determining when an artificial or biological system’s internal organization warrants cognitive explanation, as distinct from merely exhibiting competent behavior. A system can achieve high task performance through mechanisms that do not support the flexible, context-sensitive, generalizable information use that defines cognition. Without explicit criteria, debates about what AI systems do or do not instantiate remain unfalsifiable, and the different communities involved lack common ground for what would count as evidence either way. Developing those criteria, and the methods to apply them, is a fully research program that will be developed in the upcoming months.

Finally, in relation to decision science, computational ecology — the study of how cognitive systems are shaped by the statistical structure of their environments — will resurface for its capacity to ask what computations are adaptive given the structure of the environment the system evolved or learned in (cf. Lieder and Griffiths, 2019). The next decade will likely see this framing gain traction as a constraint on model building and a lens for cross-species comparison.

Challenges ahead

Three challenges stand out as genuinely difficult.

The first is multi-level phenotyping. Computational model parameters fit to a single task capture different processes depending on task characteristics, as Eckstein and colleagues (2022) showed. Without joint modeling across tasks, shared and context-specific processes cannot be separated, and individual-level estimates remain unreliable. The dimensionality reduction methods required to fuse behavioral, computational, genomic, and connectivity data into stable phenotypes are not yet standard, and the validation infrastructure to check whether those phenotypes generalize across contexts and time is largely absent.

The second is the persistent underuse of existing large-scale datasets. Infrastructures exist (e.g., the ABCD Study), but the adoption does not. This may reflect incentive structures that reward data collection over data analysis, or the computational and methodological barriers to re-analyzing heterogeneous datasets collected under different protocols. On the other hand, open-data and computational reproducibility practices are more encouraged than ever. With those practices, and with the possibilities in data replication offered by recent AI systems, we will observe an increasing number of small labs, likely from non-WEIRD countries, conducting research from open-data repositories.

The third challenge is cross-sector alignment. The neurotech industry is currently organized around a very different set of priorities than the research enterprise — consumer EEG headsets, portable monitoring devices, and applications aimed at focus and mental state tracking represent an underwhelming translation of what circuit neuroscience actually understands. There is a gap between what the research community knows about circuit-level precision and what reaches clinical or consumer application. This gap can be explained by a coordination problem, involving mismatched timelines, different definitions of validation, unresolved questions about data ownership and access, and monetary incentives. Building the translational pathways the BRAIN Roadmap envisions will require bridging these differences.

The past few years have seen the development of many tools and methods to produce better data integration and translation. Time to get a move on.

If the field succeeds, we obtain precision psychiatry that addresses cognitive heterogeneity, circuit therapies targeted to individuals, neural prosthetics for neurally impaired populations and neurotech support for cognitively impaired populations, better hopes for prevention alongside treatment, and AI architectures built on biological efficiency.

Leave a comment

References (WIP)

Benrimoh, D., Fisher, V., Mourgues, C., Sheldon, A. D., Smith, R., & Powers, A. R. (2023). Barriers and solutions to the adoption of translational tools for computational psychiatry. Molecular Psychiatry, 28(6), 2189–2196.

Brette, R. (2019). Is coding a relevant metaphor for the brain? Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 42, e215.
